title: Caching Strategies
summary: Cache-aside, write-through, write-behind, eviction policies, thundering herd, and Redis patterns.

sections.content:
## Why Cache?

Caching stores frequently-accessed data in a fast store (memory) to avoid repeated expensive operations (DB queries, API calls, computations).

**Numbers to know:**
- L1 cache: ~1ns
- Main memory (RAM): ~100ns
- SSD: ~100μs
- Network (same DC): ~500μs
- HDD: ~10ms
- Remote DB query: ~1-10ms

A cache hit (RAM) is **10,000x faster** than a remote DB query.

sections.title: Cache-Aside Pattern (Most Common)
sections.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant App
    participant Cache as Redis Cache
    participant DB
    App->>Cache: GET user:123
    alt Cache HIT
        Cache-->>App: {user data}
    else Cache MISS
        Cache-->>App: null
        App->>DB: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=123
        DB-->>App: {user data}
        App->>Cache: SET user:123 {data} EX 3600
        App-->>App: return data
    end

## Caching Strategies

### Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading)
- App checks cache first; on miss, loads from DB and populates cache
- ✓ Only caches what's actually used
- ✗ Cache miss penalty; possible stale data

### Write-Through
- Every write goes to both cache AND DB synchronously
- ✓ Cache always up to date
- ✗ Write latency; caches data that may never be read

### Write-Behind (Write-Back)
- Write to cache immediately, async write to DB later
- ✓ Fast writes
- ✗ Data loss risk if cache crashes before flush

### Read-Through
- Cache sits in front of DB; on miss, cache loads from DB itself
- ✓ App code stays simple
- ✗ First request always misses

sections.title: Write-Through vs Write-Behind
sections.content:
graph LR
    subgraph Write-Through
        App1[App] -->|write| C1[Cache]
        C1 -->|sync write| DB1[DB]
        DB1 -->|ack| C1
        C1 -->|ack| App1
    end
    subgraph Write-Behind
        App2[App] -->|write| C2[Cache]
        C2 -->|ack immediately| App2
        C2 -->|async later| DB2[DB]
    end
    style C1 fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style C2 fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Eviction Policies

When cache is full, old entries must be evicted:

| Policy | Description | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **LRU** (Least Recently Used) | Evict item not used for longest time | General purpose |
| **LFU** (Least Frequently Used) | Evict item used least often | Skewed access patterns |
| **TTL** (Time To Live) | Evict after fixed time | Time-sensitive data |
| **FIFO** | Evict oldest item | Simple queue-like scenarios |
| **Random** | Evict random item | Low overhead approximation |

Redis default: `allkeys-lru`. Set per-key TTL:
```bash
SET user:123 {data} EX 3600   # expires in 1 hour
EXPIRE user:123 7200          # reset TTL
TTL user:123                  # check remaining TTL
```

sections.title: Cache Stampede vs Cache Penetration vs Cache Avalanche
sections.content: Cache Stampede (Thundering Herd): one expired key causes many DB hits. Cache Penetration: queries for keys that will never exist in cache (e.g. IDs that don't exist in DB) — use Bloom filter. Cache Avalanche: many keys expire simultaneously — use jittered TTLs (TTL + random offset).

sections.question: What is the thundering herd problem in caching?
options: When too many cache servers are added simultaneously
When a popular cache entry expires and many concurrent requests simultaneously query the database
When cache eviction removes entries that are still needed
When write-through cache writes overwhelm the database
sections.explanation: The thundering herd (or cache stampede) occurs when a hot cache entry expires. All requests that were being served from cache now simultaneously hit the database, which wasn't designed to handle that sudden spike. Solutions include distributed locks, background refresh, or stale-while-revalidate patterns.
